sentences.text: Well, I think a fair amount of this developed in the IMT case-- again, shocked.
First major case.
The major case.
And I don't think-- the stuff was-- was in the Reichsbank at the end of the war, I think, or had been removed there to some place in Saxony.

There were pictures and stuff, in the Reichsbank And Puhl-- one of the interesting things is, when you got down to it, the individual defendants never denied the events that took place, the conduct, the transactions.
What they denied was their individual responsibility.
By saying-- it really varied from case to case-- but in some instances, it was, well, you know, I was under orders given to me by dangerous people.
I couldn't do anything but obey-- Superior Orders Doctrine.
In some cases it was, I was a mitigating factor.
Look, if someone else had the job instead of me, we would have done worse things.
There were a variety of denials.
In some cases, they attempted to deny that they had anything to do with the transaction.
And then, of course, you'd produce documentary records which showed that they were involved.
Lying was endemic.
It was a standard Nazi technique.
The Nazi party was founded on lies, so why wouldn't they lie individually?
Mitigation, maybe, if you could establish it.
But the kind of mitigation which was attempted in my case was kind of hopeless.
No, it was axiomatic at Nuremberg that mitigation was no defense.
It might be Superior Orders was no defense, it might be mitigation.
That's all.
It was my general assessment across the board, namely that they were liars.
It was a panel of three judges that heard the ministry's case.
No, no, there were separate panels for each of the 12 cases.
Yeah.
They were all American.
The prosecution in theory was all American, although we had conjoined a handful of foreign lawyers, too.
By and large I thought they were pretty good, with some exceptions.
Some of them were in the pockets of-- I won't go as far to say sympathetic to the Nazis, but they certainly weren't all that hostile to them.
They just didn't think these were big deals-- I mean, killing Jews and seizing Jewish property, what's the matter with that?
But that was decidedly a minority position.
Some of the judges were damn good.
And across the board, of course, although there were a fair number of acquittals in the subsequent proceedings, by and large, the defendants were convicted about 75%.
And this was in an atmosphere that had totally changed.
The war was over.
It was a Cold War atmosphere-- the American press covering the trials were interested only in anti-Russian references.
They would pick up anything the Germans had to say that was anti-Russian, and that was the only thing that was interesting about the case.
We got consistent press coverage from a fellow named Edwin Hartridge, who was always at Nuremberg, who covered it for when existed.
And he was the most consistent.
He thought the Nuremberg Trials were an anti-communist venture-- and that was the only thing that was interesting to him.
He also leaned somewhat sympathetically to the defendants who were being persecuted.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In other words, I didn't much care for Mr.
Hartridge's reports.
No, no.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was-- well, this post IMT, which is the four-powered international tribunal of the major criminals.
By this time, we had a small Czech liaison office in Nuremberg, we had a couple of Yugoslavs who were fairly ostracized because the United States had broken relations with Yugoslavia in this period.
We had some Dutch liaison office.
We had some of the remains of what had been the British staff at the big trial.
The Russians were nowhere in evidence-- nothing, that is the Soviets.
That was about it.
Documentary, very heavily documentary.
I mean, as I said, I had 1,000 documents translated at hand during the trial.
I actually introduced several hundred.
I had, I don't know, something like 10, 12 document books full of documents.
I can't remember anything-- the-- the only thing that I remember very distinctly is the endless lies of Rasch.
I remember a commission hearing with some young girl who testified on behalf of Puhl, and she was the most difficult witness I ever dealt with because she was loyal to the hilt.
I think she was a housemaid-- and smart.
And I do have a vague impression of her as a very tenacious witness whom I did not make any headway with.
No, they weren't convincing.
I think Rasch's lies convicted him.
Yeah.
Somebody sat up with earphones, picked up the-- the questioning was usually done in English.
The witness would hear it in German.
It would be translated by an interpreter sitting in a booth.
And the answers would come back in German, and you'd get them in English.
By the-- by this time, of course, while the English interpretation simultaneously was helpful, I could pick up a fair amount on direct listening.
It was extremely good.
These were people who were fluent in both languages.
Most of the interpreters were German or Austrian born.
German was their first language, but they had lived in the States or England long enough so that they were perfectly fluent in English.
Huh?
Oh, yeah, there was-- there was very little quibbling about the interpretations.
The German defense counsel had-- had their own interpreters listening in.
There was more dispute in the original trial.
He was convicted basically on these so-called spoliation or plunder charges, and as an accessory before or after murder.
That was what he was convicted for.
The financing concentration camp charges failed.
The courts didn't take them seriously.
What was his sentence?
He got an eight-year sentence with time off for time spent in jail during the course of the proceedings, which was roughly three and a half, four years.
John J.
McCloy, that distinguished, High Commissioner for Germany, appointed, I guess, by the Truman administration, commuted all the sentences, effectively.
Including Rasch?
Oh yeah, sure.
Why not?
I mean, let's not be nasty to Germans.
Huh?
No, I never had that privilege.
Two things-- American policy, which was to conciliate the Germans and oppose the Russians, and second, the prospect on behalf of his New York law firm getting some German business.
I won't name it.
Huh?
What
All commuted.
Oh yeah, all my cases were convictions.
You were never in doubt of that, were you?
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yes.

There was never any-- look, in the Nuremberg Trials, if you were responsible for a half million murders or more, let's say the overall commandant for all the concentration camps-- Oswald Pohl, you would get a death sentence.
If you were responsible for 100,000 up to a half million, it was maybe a 20-year sentence.
You got below 100,000, that was negligible.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's not quite accurate, but that's roughly the scale.
Otto Ohlendorf was head of the Einsatzgruppen, Now, you may not know what a Einsatzgruppen group is.
Well, let's assume you do.
But I would translate it roughly as SS murder squads.
That was their duty.

I suppose he was instrumental in killing about 200,000 or 300,000 people in the fields-- these were the detachments that machine gunned people, men women, and children over open pits so they could bury them easily.
He got a death sentence.
But a lot of people who were involved in miscellaneous murders here and there just got terms, were out of jail about as fast as I got back to the States.
As a matter of fact, it was very interesting.
I ought to amplify that a little.
While I was there, at the end of the trials, I was there practically to the very end.
When the cases were all concluded, and I was just loafing around-- I was entitled to about two months compensatory leave time, because I hadn't taken all that much leave.
He must have been 80 years old at that time.
He was a stateless Jew from Freiburg, who had been a professor of languages at a German university, and who was used by a bunch of Jewish DP's for currency transactions.
He could legally deal in script and American money.
The DP's were prohibited.
But they were kind of connivers, and managed to secure a lot of script.
And he was able to convert it for them.
But I finally relented, and I took on the defense of this guy-- who was, as I say, stateless.
It was a court trial by the occupation court.
It was not a court martial.
But with a very nebulous, unstated charge.
Because what he was really charged with was fronting for a group that was charged with undermining the German economy.
Well, I can tell you, these people wanted to undermine the German economy.
The German economy was an economy they knew very well.
They had functioned as slave laborers for four years for it.
This guy was actually used.
He didn't know what the hell he was doing.
He didn't make any money, or anything else.
He was used by a bunch of young-- young kids from the DP camps.
So the first thing we did was we raised a couple bucks here and there around the court house from the staff that was left.
We applied to get him out on bail, and we were successful, surprisingly.
Who was the judge?
Some lawyer from California, working for the occupation.
So as soon as we got him out of jail, I told him, the first thing you do is get your rear end to Switzerland and stay there-- which he could do.
And he did.
So the trial then went on, on a breach of bail-- and they confiscated the $300 or so that we put up.
They tried him.
They found him guilty of conduct unbecoming.
There were no-- no such charge.
This is a-- an assimilated army charge, conduct unbecoming an officer and gentleman-- which is very nebulous in military law.
But they convicted him.
They gave him a five-year sentence, which was almost as much as any defendant at the Nuremberg Trials.
Oh, yeah, yeah.
Different attitude.
Five years for undermining the German economy.
Of course, he never came back to Germany.
He died in Switzerland.
Oh, yeah.
I don't know what the sentences were.
One of these defendants got an eight-year sentence, and one got a 15-year sentence.
But the real result was that they were both out of jail pretty quickly.
And Puhl, I think, became a member of the board of the Dresdner Bank-- and I think he owed his subsequent career to me, because I put him in the same dock.
Probably three months or so, but a fair amount of the time was taken up with the case of this stateless--
Translator, yeah.
Oh, Werber?
Yeah, Werber was a-- strictly devoted to working on these cases when I knew him.
Very friendly.
Yeah, he was basically an investigator and an interpreter for me on the bank cases.
Subsequently, he moved back to Vienna, which was his native place.
He then became a spy for the Russians-- along with a fair number of people who worked as interpreters at Nuremberg, who became spies for the CIA.
They used to deal with each other, you know , dicker.

And at one time, Werber must have gotten out of the accomplished negotiations, because I picked up a morning newspaper-- I was working in Washington with a law firm then, Paul Weiss-- and the big headline, 'Germans Brought to the States for Espionage Charges.' And I took a look at it, and I started to read the article, and there was Werber, and his brother-in-law, who was also at Nuremberg.
And I thought, uh-oh, I'm now in for it, because the only lawyer he knows in Washington is me.
So I went to my office, and by noon I had a call from the jail.

This was a hiatus, quiet period during the Cold War, and it was the custom during quiet periods of the Cold War to heat them up-- because you had to have a constant anti-communist public opinion in the United States.
That was necessary.

Normally in this espionage center, which was Vienna, I mean, where about 1/3 third of the total population engaged in nothing but spying, you would try people before court martials, they would get a two-month sentence and that was it.
But they decided to make a showcase trial out of Werber.
So they brought him here and put him on trial.
I got a call from the jail.
I didn't know anything about local criminal practice, and so I went down to the jail to talk to him.
There are all kinds of ways of handling these things where you don't go to the jail and talk to them-- but I didn't know any of the-- any of the ropes.
I was a tax lawyer by this time, through Charlie Lyons, who had become assistant attorney general of the tax division.
And I was working for Randolph Paul, who was a famous tax lawyer, and who was involved in some libel case as a lawyer, defending Wechsler of Walter Winchell case.
And it was very awkward to, in any way, represent anybody charged with anything communist.
I went down to see Werber, and I said, what are the charges?
What's it all about?
He says, oh, I'm innocent.
And he started to give me a story which was concocted, which was transparently false.
Among other things, he claimed he did some work for Jewish agencies.
He was strictly-- he was Jewish, but he wasn't connected with anything Jewish.
He was connected with the Communist Party.
He had been a member of the Communist Party from the age of 14 on.

His wife immediately sued him for an annulment, although they had two kids by this time had been married about 10 years, on the ground that he deceived her as to his political beliefs-- which worked in New York.
These were the days when you only got divorced by annulment.
She was a member of the Communist Party from the age of 14, too.
Anyhow, I-- I talked to Werber at some length and he-- he let me off the hook.
I was really in trouble with him, because he started by wanting me as counsel.
But he had this tremendously concocted story, which he was prepared to represent in court.
And I told him I would not represent him on what I knew to be a bunch of lies, that's all.
So we finally parted ways.
And I said, under extreme circumstances, if he couldn't get a decent appointment, I'd still come back and represent him, because I was a friend of his.
And he then got Roger Robb appointed.
Robb subsequently is a court of the US Court of Appeals here, and was notoriously conservative and right wing.

And he got along very well with Robb, and Robb was prepared to defend him to the hilt on this concocted story until, I think, one day the prosecution took Robb aside, showed him photographs of Werber here, there, and elsewhere.
They had built up a pretty complete case so Robb finally pleaded him guilty and Werber got, I think, something like an eight or 10-year sentence.
Sure.
I saw him when he went to jail.
He had problems with respect to the custody of his kids.
His wife, who got this phony annulment, had the kids who grew up and finally went to law school in the United States.
When Werber finished his prison term, which is about six years later at Lewisburg, he went back to Vienna.
He could never enter the United States again.
He's always been barred.
He won't be at the reunion.
And I then traveled one year to Vienna, and I called him up because to hell with all the political background-- it had nothing really to do with me.
And he was an old friend.
And he-- he had remarried, he was now married to some German-Austrian woman, who was an interpreter of a communist conferences-- Russian, Czech, whatever.
And I was staying in a hotel in some outlying section of Vienna, and he said he'd come by and pick us up for dinner.
And his daughter from Virginia, who lived in Virginia, was visiting at the same time.
He comes by in a Jaguar convertible.
[LAUGHS] Living it up, I mean, obviously.
He then took us all to dinner in some monastery, which was a very good restaurant, excellent food in Vienna.
And it was very lavish.
I asked him what the hell he was doing, and I suspect that during these years he was doing the same thing.
He was still being a communist spy.
He was now an international tax expert, with a background in chemistry.
And he used to travel all over Europe.
And I figured that was some kind of official prescribed duty on behalf of his masters.
As far as I know, he is.
Have I been in touch with him since?
No, I haven't really seen him-- maybe the last time was 15 years ago.
But I wouldn't hesitate to go see him.
By now he must be extremely chagrined, you know.
The whole Russian system has collapsed-- he spent six years in jail for nothing.
He was pretty disenchanted when I saw him in Vienna-- even if he was still working for the same people.
He just wasn't as convinced a communist.
But he was pretty skillful at Nuremberg.
He was obviously a communist then, he was a communist from the age of 14 on.
But we'd go out, I remember traveling to Prague with him-- with another one of our colleagues.
Three of us were sitting at lunch, I do remember this vividly.
And the other fellow was pretty conservative and right wing, probably went to work for the CIA.
And I was leaning left at that time-- not communist, but left, you know, certainly anti-Nazi to a higher degree than most of the people around.
And Werber was the middle man, which was kind of funny, you know, because it was camouflage.
I, who had no communist affiliations whatever, was representing a kind of a leftist view and he was a great moderator between left and right.
I came back to the States March or April of '49.
Much later in the year.
It was August, end of August.
My wife-- in Montreal.
My wife emigrated from Germany to Canada, where she worked for the Canadian Jewish Congress.
Interpreter.
She spoke some-- I didn't know it-- but she spoke some Russian, she spoke-- by this time, she spoke pretty good Yiddish.
No, I had to bring her in.
It was very difficult.
She had broken her arm in an automobile accident, and the American consular officials wouldn't let her cross the border with a broken arm.
So I had to sit in the consular office for three days in order to get her across the border.
Oh, yeah, she became a US citizen promptly.
Yup.
I came straight home and I thought I was really in for it.
I'd have to go into private practice, which was no part of my thinking.
And I was in touch with Charlie Lyon, whom I mentioned at Nuremberg, who was a tax lawyer by background, with a rather well-known Washington firm.
And he said, why don't you go into tax practice?
I said, don't be crazy.
I never even took a tax course in law school-- which I didn't.
Which, at Harvard, was unusual, because 95%, 99% of the class took tax courses.
Not me, because I was never going to go anywhere near it.
And I haven't even filled in a tax return up to that point, as far as I can remember.
I knew nothing.
He said, I have some friends.
They might want to hire you.
I went up and talked to his friends and one of them had a commitment to write a book so he hired-- on taxes-- so he hired me basically to write a book on taxes.
Well, we never wrote a book on taxes.
I worked on one chapter and then I gradually got integrated into the tax practice itself, and then I was ruined for life.
I was a tax lawyer.
[LAUGHS]
I was with this relatively smaller firm for about a year or two, and then I was-- became an associative at Paul Weiss in New York.
From Paul Weiss in New York, I went to Paul Weiss in Washington-- which was where Randolph Paul was.
Randolph Paul was the Paul of Paul Weiss.
I spent three years in Washington, then became a partner in a Chicago firm that represented Sears Roebuck, Whirlpool corporation, a number of-- I actually incorporated Sears Roebuck.
No, it was in Chicago after I'd been practicing for four years that I became a partner.
To a number of other Chicago firms.
12 Years.
And then I went to Arnold and Porter as a partner, and I've been there ever since.
Yeah.
Yup.
All right.
